sections.markdown:
# DEFGENERIC and DEFMETHOD

A **generic function** is a function whose behaviour is selected at call time based on the **types** of its arguments. You declare it once with `defgeneric`, then add **methods** with `defmethod`.

```lisp
(defgeneric area (shape))

(defmethod area ((s circle))
  (* pi (expt (circle-radius s) 2)))

(defmethod area ((s rect))
  (* (rect-w s) (rect-h s)))
```

When you call `(area some-shape)`, CLOS picks the most specific applicable method based on the actual class of `some-shape`.

Key points:

* `defgeneric` is optional — adding the first `defmethod` will create the generic function — but writing the `defgeneric` makes the contract explicit and is good documentation.
* The specialiser `(s circle)` says "this method applies when the first argument is a `circle`". An unspecialised parameter (`s`) means "matches anything".
* Specialisers may be class names OR `(eql object)` — see lesson 053.
* You can specialise more than one parameter — that's **multiple dispatch** (lesson 052).

A generic function is a single Lisp function, owned by no class. Methods are how classes plug *into* it. This is one of the things that makes CLOS feel different from class-based OO in Java/Python.

sections.code: (progn (defclass animal () ()) (defclass dog (animal) ()) (defclass cat (animal) ()) (defgeneric speak (a)) (defmethod speak ((a dog)) :woof) (defmethod speak ((a cat)) :meow) (list (speak (make-instance 'dog)) (speak (make-instance 'cat))))
sections.explain: Each class plugs its own method into the SPEAK generic function.

sections.code: (progn (defclass animal () ()) (defclass dog (animal) ()) (defgeneric speak (a)) (defmethod speak ((a animal)) :generic) (defmethod speak ((a dog)) :woof) (speak (make-instance 'dog)))
sections.explain: Most specific method wins. DOG is more specific than ANIMAL.

sections.code: (progn (defgeneric describe-it (x)) (defmethod describe-it ((x integer)) :int) (defmethod describe-it ((x string)) :str) (defmethod describe-it (x) (declare (ignore x)) :other) (list (describe-it 5) (describe-it "hi") (describe-it 'sym)))
sections.explain: Methods can specialise on built-in classes (INTEGER, STRING) too. Unspecialised parameter is the catch-all.

sections.prompt: Define a class SHAPE, and subclasses CIRCLE (slot RADIUS, accessor radius-of) and SQUARE (slot SIDE, accessor side-of). Define a generic function PERIMETER and methods that compute (* 2 pi r) and (* 4 side). Tests use exact integer side and check perimeter for SQUARE only (avoid float comparisons).
sections.starter:
(defclass shape () ())
(defclass circle (shape) ((radius :initarg :radius :accessor radius-of)))
(defclass square (shape) ((side :initarg :side :accessor side-of)))

(defgeneric perimeter (s))
sections.hint: (defmethod perimeter ((s square)) (* 4 (side-of s))) and analogously for CIRCLE.
